scenario: Apex Fintech is reviewing the BPMN XML of a Camunda 8.8 payment process that must catch an unknown error code from a job worker for 4500 transactions per hour. The reviewer focuses on the boundary event XML attached to the collect-money task in the same scope of the model.
question: Which XML structure correctly defines an error catch-all boundary event on the collect-money task in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition that references an errorRef pointing to a bpmn:error definition with an empty errorCode attribute on the error
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition that has no errorRef attribute, so the boundary event catches every thrown error code reaching this scope
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with a generic eventDefinition element and a special errorCode attribute set to the wildcard value of asterisk on the bpmn:error element
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition referencing a bpmn:error whose errorCode attribute is the wildcard value of asterisk in the diagram

a.text: Incorrect. The Additional resources XML shows the catch-all variant with an errorEventDefinition that has no errorRef at all; an errorRef pointing at an empty errorCode is not the documented pattern in BPMN XML.

b.text: Correct. The Additional resources section shows an error catch-all boundary event whose errorEventDefinition element is present without any errorRef attribute, so it catches every thrown error in scope of the boundary.

c.text: Incorrect. The XML uses bpmn:errorEventDefinition, not a generic eventDefinition with a wildcard errorCode attribute; catch-all behaviour comes from omitting errorRef on the errorEventDefinition.

d.text: Incorrect. The page defines catch-all behaviour by omitting errorRef on the errorEventDefinition, not by using an asterisk errorCode on a referenced bpmn:error in the BPMN XML representation.

explanation: An error catch-all boundary event in BPMN XML is expressed by an errorEventDefinition with no errorRef attribute attached to the task via attachedToRef. The catch event then matches any thrown error within its scope hierarchy.
